walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Board and Basic Components
walkthrough.text: Welcome to the Valley, farmer. Let's get everything set up so you can start your new life. Place the big board in the center of the table where everyone can see it. Set the Stardew Dice (the three custom dice with Heart, Junimo, and Stardrop faces), the Animal Dice, and the Spouse Pawn nearby. Now shuffle each of these decks separately and place them face down near the board: Villagers, Items, Epic Items, Events, and Mine Events. Also shuffle the stack of Joja Tiles and set them face down.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Tile Tray and Resource Bags
walkthrough.text: Find the Tile Tray, sometimes called the Supply. This holds all your resource tiles: crops, animal products, bug meat, stones, ore, and geodes. Place it near the board where everyone can reach it. There are also two cloth bags. Put all the Artifact and Mineral tiles into the gray bag, and all the Fish tiles into the blue Fishing Bag. You will draw from these bags throughout the game whenever you go mining or fishing.

walkthrough.title: Plant Your First Crop
walkthrough.text: Every farm needs a humble beginning. Find the Parsnip tile from the Spring Crops in the Tile Tray and place it in field slot number 2 on the board, normal side up. Crops are double-sided: the front is normal and the back is the Quality star version. Always plant them normal side up. This little parsnip is your very first step toward a thriving farm.

walkthrough.title: Place Forageables, Trees, and Fish
walkthrough.text: Scatter the 11 Spring Forageable tiles face down onto the Foraging Spots on the board. These are the little circles along the paths. Then place the 4 Spring Tree tiles leaf-side up onto the Tree Spots. Keep the remaining seasons' forageables and trees nearby for later. Next, draw 5 random tiles from the Fishing Bag and place 1 in each slot on the Fish Track, filling from right to left. The Valley is teeming with life already.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Mine
walkthrough.text: Sort the Mine Level cards by number so Level 1 is on top and Level 12 is on the bottom. Place this stack face up on the Mine space on the board. Then shuffle the Map Card deck, draw 1 card, and place it face up next to the Mine Levels. This Map Card is a grid that determines what you find each time you explore the Mine. The deeper you go, the better the resources.

walkthrough.title: Build the Season Deck
walkthrough.text: This is the heartbeat of the game, so take a moment here. For your first game, find the 16 Standard Season Cards (marked with a special icon in the bottom right). Separate them into 4 piles by season: Spring, Summer, Fall, and Winter. Shuffle each pile separately, then stack each pile of 4 on top of its matching Season End Card, face down. Now stack all four seasonal piles together with Spring on top, then Summer, Fall, and Winter on the bottom. Place this Season Deck on the board. Each card drawn is one day of your year in the Valley.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Community Center and Grandpa's Goals
walkthrough.text: The Community Center has 6 rooms on the board: Crafts Room, Pantry, Fish Tank, Boiler Room, Vault, and Bulletin Board. For each room, draw 1 random Bundle card that matches and place it face down on that room without peeking. Set aside the unused Bundles. Then shuffle the Goal Deck and draw 4 Goal cards. Place them face up on Grandpa's Letter at the top of the board. These are the 4 goals your team must complete to make Grandpa proud.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Animals and Buildings
walkthrough.text: Create 2 stacks of Animal tiles near the board, separated by type: Coop animals and Barn animals. Place the Coop Building tile on top of the Coop stack, and the Barn Building tile on top of the Barn stack. Then randomly draw 2 additional Building tiles and place them beside the Coop and Barn. Return any remaining Buildings to the box. You will need Wood, Stone, and Gold to construct these later.

walkthrough.title: Choose Professions and Starting Tools
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a Player Mat, which determines your Profession. Take the matching colored pawn. Then each player chooses a Starting Tool Deck: Watering Can, Hoe, Fishing Rod, or Pickaxe. Stack your tool cards on your Player Mat with Level 0 face up on top. Each tool upgrades throughout the game, giving you better abilities as you invest in them. Your Player Mat also shows slots for resources, items, and Profession Upgrades.

walkthrough.title: Choose the Starting Player and Begin
walkthrough.text: Whoever recommended playing the game gets to choose the Starting Player and hand them the Pet Token. Your group also picks whether the pet is a cat or a dog, because that matters in Stardew Valley. The whole team shares a starting pool of 3 Gold. Gold and Heart Tokens are communal resources that everyone shares. Now draw the first Season Card and your year on the farm begins. Good luck, farmers.

summary.title: Lay Out the Board and Basic Components
bullets: Place the board centrally with Stardew Dice, Animal Dice, and Spouse Pawn nearby
Shuffle and place face down: Villagers, Items, Epic Items, Events, Mine Events, Joja Tiles

summary.title: Set Up the Tile Tray and Resource Bags
bullets: Place the Tile Tray (Supply) near the board with crops, animal products, ore, geodes, bug meat, stones
Gray bag = Artifacts & Minerals; Blue bag = Fish tiles

summary.title: Plant Your First Crop
bullets: Place 1 Parsnip (Spring Crop) in field slot 2, normal side up
Crops are double-sided: normal front, Quality star back

summary.title: Place Forageables, Trees, and Fish
bullets: 11 Spring Forageable tiles go face down on Foraging Spots
4 Spring Tree tiles go leaf-side up on Tree Spots
Draw 5 Fish tiles from the Fishing Bag and fill the Fish Track right to left

summary.title: Prepare the Mine
bullets: Stack Mine Level cards 1-12 face up (Level 1 on top)
Shuffle Map Cards, reveal 1 face up next to the Mine

summary.title: Build the Season Deck
bullets: For first game: use 16 Standard Season Cards only
Separate by season, shuffle each pile, stack on matching Season End Card
Combine: Spring on top, then Summer, Fall, Winter on bottom

summary.title: Set Up the Community Center and Grandpa's Goals
bullets: Place 1 random Bundle face down on each of the 6 Community Center rooms
Draw 4 Goal cards face up onto Grandpa's Letter

summary.title: Prepare Animals and Buildings
bullets: Stack Coop animals under Coop Building tile, Barn animals under Barn Building tile
Draw 2 random additional Building tiles; return the rest to the box

summary.title: Choose Professions and Starting Tools
bullets: Each player picks a Player Mat (Profession) and matching colored pawn
Each player chooses a Starting Tool Deck (Watering Can, Hoe, Fishing Rod, or Pickaxe)
Stack tool cards with Level 0 on top

summary.title: Choose the Starting Player and Begin
bullets: Game recommender picks Starting Player and gives them the Pet Token
Group picks cat or dog
Players share a starting pool of 3 Gold
Draw the first Season Card to begin

walkthrough.title: The Goal: Save the Valley
walkthrough.text: Before we get into the turn-by-turn details, let's talk about what you are trying to do. Stardew Valley is a cooperative game, meaning you all win or lose together. To win, you must accomplish two things before the Season Deck runs out: complete all 4 of Grandpa's Goals (shown face up at the top of the board) and restore all 6 rooms of the Community Center by completing 1 Bundle per room. If the last Season Card is drawn before both objectives are met, the Joja Corporation takes over the Valley and everyone loses. Time is precious, so plan wisely.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure: Three Phases
walkthrough.text: Each round follows three phases in order. First is the Season Phase: the Starting Player draws a Season Card and resolves its icons from top to bottom. Second is the Planning Phase: everyone discusses strategy, trades resources or items freely, and then places their pawn on any location on the board. Third is the Action Phase: starting with the player who has the Pet Token, each player takes their turn. After everyone has gone, the round ends and a new round begins with another Season Card.

walkthrough.title: Season Cards: The Rhythm of the Valley
walkthrough.text: Season Cards come in three types: Regular, Festival, and Season End. Regular cards show a row of icons that resolve top to bottom. These icons can water your crops, move the Fish Track, trigger gift abilities, send crows to eat your vegetables, summon Joja Tiles, and more. Festival cards have a single special effect written on them. Season End Cards mark the transition between seasons: when one appears, you swap out forageables and trees, each player draws 2 Profession Upgrades and keeps 1, and then you immediately draw the next Season Card and keep going. There are 4 seasons with about 5 cards each, so your year goes by fast.

walkthrough.title: Actions: What You Can Do on Your Turn
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you get exactly 2 actions. You can use them in two ways: take 2 actions at your current location with no movement, or take 1 action at your current location, move 1 space along a path, then take 1 action at your new location. That is it. Two actions, and at most one move sandwiched between them. Each location on the board offers specific actions. The Farm lets you water crops or collect from animals. The Town lets you buy seeds, make friends, or visit the Community Center. The Mountain has the Mine and building construction. And so on.

walkthrough.title: Farming: Crops and Animals
walkthrough.text: Farming is the bread and butter of Stardew Valley. To plant crops, visit Pierre's in Town, spend 1 Gold per seed, and place the crop tile directly into the field slot matching its watering number. To harvest, use the Water Crops action at the Farm to move all planted crops 1 space to the right. When a crop slides off the track, it is harvested into your inventory. Rain icons on Season Cards also water crops for free. For animals, you first need to build a Coop or Barn, then buy animals at the Ranch. Use the Collect from Animals action at the Farm to roll the Animal Dice and generate products based on which icons match your animals.

walkthrough.title: Foraging and Movement
walkthrough.text: Whenever you move along a path during the Action Phase, you may pick up 1 face-down Forageable tile or 1 Tree tile (not both) from an area bordering that path. This is free and does not cost an action. Forageables go straight into your inventory. Trees always become Wood when claimed. Some special tiles have a dig icon; when you reveal one, check the symbol, discard the tile, and draw from the Artifact and Mineral Bag to claim the specified resource. Foraging is a wonderful way to gather resources without spending your precious actions.

walkthrough.title: The Mine: Delving Deep
walkthrough.text: The Mine is at the Mountain location and is a major source of Stone, Ore, Geodes, and other surprises. Spend 1 action to roll 2 Stardew Dice, then use the current Map Card grid to determine your result. You choose which die is the row and which is the column. Results include gaining resources, drawing items, descending to the next level via a Staircase, or encountering a Monster. Monsters trigger the ability printed on the current Mine Level Card, and they get nastier as you go deeper. You can also descend by spending Stones equal to the number of players as an End of Turn Effect. Reaching Level 12 may be one of Grandpa's Goals.

walkthrough.title: Fishing: Casting Your Line
walkthrough.text: There are three fishing spots: River in the Forest, Lake at the Mountain, and Ocean at the Beach. Spend 1 action to roll all 3 Stardew Dice. Each fish on the Fish Track shows a location and catch requirements (combinations of Hearts, Junimos, and Stardrops). If your dice match the requirements of a fish at your location, you catch it. You can even catch multiple fish if you can split your dice across them. Crab Pot Fish work differently: discard 1 Bug Meat per Crab Pot Fish you want, no dice needed. If you catch nothing at all, you may grab a Trash tile from the track. Legendary Fish are extra special and never get discarded from the track by Season Card movement.

walkthrough.title: Making Friends and Giving Gifts
walkthrough.text: The villagers of Stardew Valley are charming folks, and befriending them is essential. Spend 1 action in Town to draw the top Villager card. If you gift them a resource they love, you earn 2 Heart Tokens. A liked gift (anything not pictured and not hated) earns 1 Heart. Hated resources cannot be gifted at all. If the current season matches their birthday, you earn 1 bonus Heart. Keep the Villager card as your friend. When Gift icons appear on Season Cards, each player activates 1 friend's Gift Ability, which provides ongoing bonuses. Hearts are a communal resource shared by all players and are critical for revealing Bundles.

walkthrough.title: The Community Center: Revealing and Completing Bundles
walkthrough.text: Bundles start face down, so you do not know exactly what each room needs. To reveal a Bundle, spend Heart Tokens equal to the number of players at the Community Center in Town. You can either flip the leftmost face-down Bundle or replace an already-revealed Bundle with a new one from the deck (matching the same room). To complete a Bundle, donate the specified resources a number of times equal to the player count. For example, in a 3-player game, the Spring Foraging Bundle needs 3 spring forageables. Place donated resources on the card to track progress. When complete, the room is restored, and you draw a reward: the first 5 Bundles grant regular Items, and the 6th grants an Epic Item.

walkthrough.title: End of Turn Effects
walkthrough.text: After finishing your 2 actions and returning your pawn to the Farmhouse, you may choose 1 type of End of Turn Effect and do it as many times as you can. Your options are: Build Staircase (spend Stones equal to player count to descend in the Mine), Pet Animals (spend Heart Tokens to flip an animal to its Happy side for Quality products), Remove Joja (spend 1 Heart or 5 Gold to remove a Joja Tile from the board), or Upgrade Starting Tool (discard the resource shown on your tool card to advance it one level). You pick one type and repeat it, but you cannot mix types in a single end of turn.

walkthrough.title: Joja Tiles: The Creeping Corporation
walkthrough.text: When a Joja icon appears on a Season Card, draw a Joja Tile and place it on the matching board action. Joja Tiles make that action worse or block it entirely until removed. Removing them costs 1 Heart Token or 5 Gold as an End of Turn Effect. On the standard Honest Farmer difficulty, Joja Tiles are an annoyance but not required to clear for victory. On Artisan difficulty, you must remove every Joja Tile on the board in addition to completing Goals and Bundles. Do not let Joja take over your Valley.

walkthrough.title: Inventory, Items, and Sharing
walkthrough.text: Each player's mat has 6 resource slots, 2 item slots, and 2 Profession Upgrade slots. There is no limit on Epic Items, friends, or kept Event cards. If you gain more than you can hold, you must discard something to make room. Gold and Heart Tokens are shared communally by all players with no limit. During the Planning Phase, players can freely trade resources and items, but no trading is allowed during the Action Phase. Profession Upgrades cannot be traded. Plan your trades carefully each round to make sure the right resources are in the right hands.

walkthrough.title: Winning, Losing, and Difficulty Modes
walkthrough.text: You win the instant all 4 Grandpa's Goals and all 6 Community Center Bundles are complete. You lose when the final Season Card is drawn without achieving both objectives. For an easier time, try Seedling mode where you only need to complete the 4 Goals. The standard Honest Farmer mode requires both Goals and Bundles. For a real challenge, Artisan mode adds the requirement of clearing all Joja Tiles. You can also adjust game length by using fewer Season Cards per season or revealing Bundles at the start to make things smoother. The Valley rewards patience and teamwork.

summary.title: The Goal: Save the Valley
bullets: Cooperative: all players win or lose together
Complete 4 of Grandpa's Goals AND restore all 6 Community Center Rooms
Lose if the Season Deck runs out first

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your Practice Year
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through the first few rounds of a cozy 3-player game to get everyone comfortable. Set up the game exactly as described in the setup section. For this practice, we recommend using Seedling difficulty (Goals only) so you can focus on learning the actions without the pressure of Bundles. Each player has chosen a Profession, a Starting Tool, and placed their pawn on the Farmhouse. You have 3 Gold in the communal pool. The Season Deck is built with Standard Season Cards. Ready? Let's draw that first Season Card.

walkthrough.title: Draw Your First Season Card
walkthrough.text: The Starting Player (whoever has the Pet Token) draws the top card of the Season Deck. Let's say it is a Regular Spring card showing: Rain, Gift, and Pet Wanders. Resolve each icon top to bottom. First, Rain: move all planted crops 1 space to the right. Your Parsnip moves from field 2 to field 3. Next, Gift: since nobody has friends yet, this does nothing. Finally, Pet Wanders: pass the Pet Token to the player on the left. Season Phase complete.

walkthrough.title: Planning Phase: Talk It Out
walkthrough.text: Now everyone discusses what to do this round. You only have 3 Gold and a Parsnip slowly working its way to harvest. Look at Grandpa's Goals together. Maybe one says 'Have 3 friends per player' and another says 'Reach the bottom of the Mine.' Think about who should go where. One player could head to Town to buy seeds or make a friend. Another could head to the Mountain to start exploring the Mine. Once everyone agrees, place your pawns on the locations you want to start at.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Visiting Pierre's General Store
walkthrough.text: The Starting Player placed their pawn at Town. For their first action, they choose Buy and Plant Seeds. They spend 1 Gold to buy a Cauliflower (a Spring Crop with watering cost 4) and plant it in field slot 4. For their second action, they choose Make a Friend. They draw the top Villager card. It is Leah, who loves Forageables. They do not have a Forageable to gift right now, but they could gift any non-hated resource for 1 Heart. They decide to wait and discard Leah. The player returns to the Farmhouse.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Forgetting to Gift
walkthrough.text: Hold on, that was actually a missed opportunity. When you draw a Villager, you can gift them any resource that is not hated and does not have the No Gift icon. Even if it is not their loved item, you still earn 1 Heart Token. Hearts are incredibly valuable for revealing Bundles and other effects. In the early game, it is almost always worth gifting something to keep the Villager as a friend, even if the gift is just 'liked.' Do not throw away friendship.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Heading to the Farm
walkthrough.text: Player 2 placed at the Farm. For their first action, they choose Water Crops. All planted crops move 1 space right. The Parsnip moves from field 3 to field 4, and the Cauliflower moves from field 4 to field 5. Neither has fallen off the track yet. For their second action, they Water Crops again. Now the Parsnip moves from 4 to 5 and then off the track, harvested into Player 2's inventory. The Cauliflower moves off the track too. Two crops harvested in one turn. Player 2 returns to the Farmhouse.

walkthrough.title: Wait, Clarification on Watering
walkthrough.text: Actually, let me clarify something important. Each Water Crops action moves ALL planted crops 1 space to the right simultaneously. So after the first watering, Parsnip is at field 4 (it was moved by rain to 3, now to 4) and Cauliflower is at field 5 (planted at 4, now 5). Cauliflower falls off and is harvested. Second watering moves Parsnip from 4 to 5. It does not fall off yet because field 5 is the last slot. One more watering would push it off. So only the Cauliflower was harvested this turn. Keeping track of field positions matters.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's End of Turn Effect
walkthrough.text: After returning to the Farmhouse, Player 2 may choose 1 End of Turn Effect. They look at their Starting Tool: let's say it is the Pickaxe at Level 0, which requires 1 Stone to upgrade. They do not have a Stone yet, so they skip upgrading. They could also Pet Animals (no animals yet), Remove Joja (no Joja Tiles yet), or Build Staircase (no Stones). Sometimes there is nothing to do at end of turn, and that is perfectly fine. The Valley rewards patience.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Exploring the Mine
walkthrough.text: Player 3 placed at the Mountain. For their first action, they choose Explore the Mine. They roll 2 Stardew Dice and get a Heart and a Stardrop. Looking at the Map Card grid, they choose Heart as the row and Stardrop as the column (or vice versa, whichever gives a better result). Let's say one combination gives Ore and the other gives a Monster. They wisely choose Ore and pick up a Copper Ore from the Supply (the available types are shown on the current Mine Level card).

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Second Action: Mine Again
walkthrough.text: For their second action, Player 3 explores the Mine again. This time they roll Junimo and Junimo. On the Map Card, same symbol for both row and column leads to the diagonal intersection. Let's say it is a Staircase. Wonderful. They descend to Mine Level 2. Reveal the next Mine Level Card (place it on top) and draw a new Map Card to replace the old one. The deeper they go, the more valuable the Ore and Geodes become. Player 3 returns to the Farmhouse.

walkthrough.title: Round 1 Complete: Draw the Next Season Card
walkthrough.text: Everyone has taken their turn, so the round is over. The new Starting Player (who received the Pet Token during the Season Phase) draws the next Season Card. Let's say this one shows Quality Crop, Joja, and Shipping Bin. Quality Crop: flip any 1 planted crop to its Quality side. But wait, the Parsnip is still in the field, so flip it. Joja: draw a Joja Tile and place it on the matching action. Oh no, it targets Buy and Plant Seeds. Now that action is affected until someone removes the tile. Shipping Bin: players may sell resources for Gold.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Ignoring Joja Tiles
walkthrough.text: A Joja Tile just landed on Buy and Plant Seeds. New players often shrug and say 'we will deal with it later.' But Joja Tiles can snowball quickly. Each one makes a location action worse, and if you are playing on Artisan difficulty, you need to clear them all. Even on standard difficulty, a blocked action can seriously set you back. Keep an eye on the board and plan to remove Joja Tiles before they pile up. It costs 1 Heart Token or 5 Gold per tile as an End of Turn Effect.

walkthrough.title: Planning Phase: Adapting Your Strategy
walkthrough.text: Now that a Joja Tile is on the seed shop and you have a few resources, talk about your priorities. Does someone need to remove the Joja Tile? Should someone try fishing to work toward a fishing Bundle? Who can make friends for Hearts? Planning together is the heart of this cooperative game. Place your pawns thoughtfully. Remember: you can change your mind about which specific action to take once your turn starts, but you cannot change your starting location (except by moving 1 space on your turn).

walkthrough.title: Trying Out Fishing
walkthrough.text: Let's say Player 1 moves to the Beach to try Ocean Fishing. They spend their action and roll all 3 Stardew Dice: Heart, Heart, Junimo. They look at the Fish Track. There is a Sardine in an Ocean slot requiring 1 Heart. They spend 1 die (Heart) to catch the Sardine. The remaining dice show Heart and Junimo. There is also an Ocean fish requiring exactly Heart + Junimo. They catch that one too with their remaining dice. Two fish with one action. Not bad for a newcomer.

walkthrough.title: Understanding the Fish Track Refill
walkthrough.text: After fishing, slide any remaining tiles on the Fish Track to the right to fill gaps, then draw new tiles from the Fishing Bag to fill the empty slots on the left. This means the Fish Track is always full of 5 tiles at the start of each fishing action. New fish come in from the left, and the rightmost fish get pushed off (discarded) when the Fish Track moves from Season Card icons. Legendary Fish are the exception: they return to the Fishing Bag instead of being discarded.

walkthrough.title: Making Your First Friend
walkthrough.text: Player 2 heads to Town and spends an action to Make a Friend. They draw Harvey from the Villager Deck. Harvey loves Coffee (an item, not a resource) and hates Jelly. Player 2 has a Cauliflower in their inventory, which is not loved or hated. They gift it anyway for 1 Heart Token. Harvey is now their friend and goes beside their Player Mat. When a Gift icon appears on future Season Cards, Player 2 can use Harvey's Gift Ability. Small friendships lead to big things in the Valley.

walkthrough.title: Visiting the Community Center
walkthrough.text: Player 2 still has a second action. They choose Reveal and Donate to Bundles. To reveal a Bundle, they need to spend Hearts equal to the player count (3 in this game). The group has earned some Hearts from befriending Harvey and earlier play. They spend 3 Hearts to flip the Crafts Room Bundle face up. It requires Spring Forageables. Now everyone knows what that room needs. If it had required something impossible, they could spend 3 more Hearts later to replace it with a different Crafts Room Bundle.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Hoarding Resources
walkthrough.text: Here is something that trips up new players. You only have 6 resource slots on your Player Mat. If your inventory is full when you gain a new resource, you must discard something. Do not hoard resources thinking you might need them someday. Sell extras for Gold at the Shipping Bin or Pierre's, donate them to Bundles, or gift them to villagers. Every resource sitting in your inventory that is not being used toward a goal is a wasted opportunity. Keep your inventory lean and purposeful.

walkthrough.title: End of Spring: Season End Card
walkthrough.text: After a few more rounds, you draw a Season End Card for Spring. Follow its instructions: remove all Spring Forageable tiles and Tree tiles from the board. Place Summer Forageables face down on the Foraging Spots and Summer Trees on the Tree Spots. Then each player draws 2 Profession Upgrade cards matching their Profession, looks at them, and keeps 1 (discarding 1 if they already have 2). These Upgrades give you powerful abilities throughout the rest of the game. Finally, draw the next Season Card immediately and begin the next round.

walkthrough.title: Midgame Check: How Are Your Goals Looking?
walkthrough.text: By mid-Summer you should have a sense of which Goals are on track and which need attention. Check the 4 Goals. 'Have 3 friends per player' means you need 9 friends total for 3 players. 'Reach the bottom of the Mine' means getting to Level 12. 'Have 10 Gold per player' means 30 Gold total. Goals scale with player count, so divide the work. Talk about who is best positioned for each goal based on their Profession and tool. The clock is ticking, but the Valley is generous to those who plan together.

walkthrough.title: Upgrading Your Starting Tool
walkthrough.text: Let's say Player 3 has collected some Stones and Ore throughout the game. At the end of their turn, they choose the Upgrade Starting Tool effect. Their Pickaxe is at Level 0 and requires 1 Copper Ore to advance. They discard the Copper Ore and flip to Level 1, which grants a better ability when mining. Each level requires a different resource. Upgrading your tool twice per player is one of the possible Grandpa's Goals, so do not neglect this. The higher-level abilities can be game-changing.

walkthrough.title: The Marriage Mechanic
walkthrough.text: Here is a charming detail. If you obtain the Mermaid's Pendant Epic Item (from completing Bundles or certain events), you can attach it to one of your Villager friends who has the marriage icon. You are now married. Take the Spouse Pawn. During the Planning Phase, you place the Spouse Pawn on any location. During your turn, your Spouse takes 1 action at their location, either before or after your 2 actions. They use your inventory and abilities. It is like having a bonus third action. The Spouse cannot trigger End of Turn Effects, but this is still tremendously powerful.

walkthrough.title: The Final Push: Winter
walkthrough.text: By Winter, the Season Deck is running thin. Count your remaining cards. Check your Goals. Count your completed Bundles. This is when cooperative communication is most critical. Maybe you need 2 more friends and 1 more Bundle. Assign tasks clearly: one player befriends villagers, another gathers Bundle donations, the third removes a pesky Joja Tile. Use the Shipping Bin and Pierre's to convert unneeded resources to Gold if that is a Goal. Every action counts now.

walkthrough.title: Victory or Defeat
walkthrough.text: If at any point during the game all 4 of Grandpa's Goals are complete and all 6 Community Center Rooms are restored, you win immediately. Grandpa would be so proud. The Valley thrives, the Community Center is saved, and Joja Corporation slinks away. If the final Season Card is drawn and you have not achieved both objectives, the Joja Corporation moves in and the magic fades. Either way, the journey was worth it. The Valley has a way of making every game feel like a story worth telling.

walkthrough.title: Ready for a Real Game
walkthrough.text: You have now seen the full arc of a Stardew Valley year. You know how to farm, fish, mine, forage, befriend villagers, reveal Bundles, and manage Joja Tiles. For your first real game, we recommend Honest Farmer difficulty with 3 players and Standard Season Cards. As you get more comfortable, try Optional Season Cards for more variety, or Artisan mode for a real challenge. The Valley has so much to discover, and no two games are ever quite the same. Good luck, farmer. Grandpa is watching.

summary.title: Welcome to Your Practice Year
bullets: 3-player practice game on Seedling difficulty recommended
Standard Season Cards, all players start at Farmhouse with 3 shared Gold

summary.title: Draw Your First Season Card
bullets: Starting Player draws and resolves icons top to bottom
Rain waters crops (move 1 right), Gift triggers friend abilities, Pet Wanders passes token

summary.title: Planning Phase: Talk It Out
bullets: Review Grandpa's Goals together and divide responsibilities
Discuss, trade, then place pawns on starting locations

summary.title: Player 1: Visiting Pierre's General Store
bullets: Buy & Plant Seeds: 1 Gold per crop, placed in field matching watering number
Make a Friend: draw a Villager and decide whether to gift a resource

summary.title: Common Mistake: Forgetting to Gift
bullets: Even a 'liked' gift earns 1 Heart Token
Hearts are critical for revealing Bundles; almost always worth gifting something

summary.title: Player 2: Heading to the Farm
bullets: Water Crops moves ALL planted crops 1 space right simultaneously
Crops falling off the track are harvested into your inventory

summary.title: Wait, Clarification on Watering
bullets: Each watering moves all crops 1 space, not just one crop
Track field positions carefully; field 5 is the last slot before harvest

summary.title: Player 2's End of Turn Effect
bullets: Choose 1 type of effect after returning to Farmhouse: Staircase, Pet, Remove Joja, or Upgrade Tool
It is okay to skip if nothing applies yet

summary.title: Player 3: Exploring the Mine
bullets: Roll 2 Stardew Dice, choose which is row and which is column on Map Card
Pick the result that helps you most

summary.title: Player 3's Second Action: Mine Again
bullets: Staircase result = descend to next Mine Level + draw new Map Card
Deeper levels have better Ore and Geodes but scarier Monsters

summary.title: Round 1 Complete: Draw the Next Season Card
bullets: New Starting Player draws next card
Quality Crop flips a planted crop; Joja places a tile; Shipping Bin allows selling

summary.title: Common Mistake: Ignoring Joja Tiles
bullets: Joja Tiles block or weaken board actions; they snowball if left alone
Remove early: 1 Heart or 5 Gold per tile (End of Turn Effect)

summary.title: Planning Phase: Adapting Your Strategy
bullets: Re-evaluate priorities each round based on board state
You can change your action choice but not your starting location

summary.title: Trying Out Fishing
bullets: Roll 3 Stardew Dice; spend symbols to match catch requirements of fish at your location
Possible to catch multiple fish per action by splitting dice

summary.title: Understanding the Fish Track Refill
bullets: After catching, slide tiles right to fill gaps, then draw from Fishing Bag for empty slots
Fish Track always has 5 tiles; Legendary Fish return to bag instead of being discarded

summary.title: Making Your First Friend
bullets: Gift any non-hated resource for at least 1 Heart
Friend stays beside your mat; Gift Ability activates on future Gift icons

summary.title: Visiting the Community Center
bullets: Reveal a Bundle: spend Hearts = player count
If a Bundle seems too hard, spend Hearts again later to replace it

summary.title: Common Mistake: Hoarding Resources
bullets: Only 6 resource slots per player; full inventory means forced discards
Sell, donate, or gift extras instead of letting them sit unused

summary.title: End of Spring: Season End Card
bullets: Swap Forageables and Trees to the new season
Each player draws 2 Profession Upgrades, keeps 1
Draw the next Season Card immediately after

summary.title: Midgame Check: How Are Your Goals Looking?
bullets: Goals scale with player count; divide labor by Profession and tool
Communicate openly about which goals are on track

summary.title: Upgrading Your Starting Tool
bullets: End of Turn Effect: discard the listed resource to advance tool 1 level
Higher levels grant stronger abilities; may be required by a Grandpa's Goal

summary.title: The Marriage Mechanic
bullets: Mermaid's Pendant Epic Item + marriageable friend = marriage
Spouse Pawn takes 1 extra action per turn at any location using your inventory

summary.title: The Final Push: Winter
bullets: Count remaining Season Cards and unfinished objectives
Assign clear tasks; every action counts in the final season

summary.title: Victory or Defeat
bullets: Win instantly when all 4 Goals + all 6 Bundles are complete
Lose when the last Season Card is drawn without achieving both

summary.title: Ready for a Real Game
bullets: Recommended first real game: 3 players, Honest Farmer, Standard Season Cards
Try Optional Season Cards and Artisan mode as you gain experience

walkthrough.title: Communicate Constantly
walkthrough.text: Stardew Valley is a cooperative game, and the Planning Phase is your most valuable tool. Talk openly about what resources you have, what you need, and where you are heading. There is no hidden information among players (except unplayed Villager cards in the deck). Share your plans, ask for help, and coordinate. A team that communicates well can accomplish twice as much as a team where everyone plays solo.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Hearts Early
walkthrough.text: Heart Tokens are the currency that unlocks the Community Center. You need them to reveal Bundles, and you need them to pet animals, remove Joja Tiles, and more. Making friends early means more Gift Abilities triggering on Season Cards, which means more resources flowing in for free. Even if 'Make Friends' is not one of your Grandpa's Goals, you still need friends to generate the Hearts that fuel so many other systems.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Neglect the Mine
walkthrough.text: The Mine provides Stone, Ore, Geodes, Bug Meat, items, and the occasional Mine Event. Stones are needed for buildings and staircases. Ore is needed for tool upgrades and certain Bundles. Geodes can be cracked open for Minerals, Artifacts, and more. Even if 'Reach the bottom of the Mine' is not one of your Goals, the resources you find down there are essential for so many other objectives. Send at least one player regularly.

walkthrough.title: Forage Whenever You Move
walkthrough.text: Foraging is free. Every time you move along a path, you get to grab a Forageable or Tree tile at no action cost. This is easy to forget in the heat of the moment, but those tiles add up quickly. Forageables can be gifted to villagers, donated to Bundles, or sold for Gold. Trees become Wood for buildings. Never waste a movement without picking something up. It is like finding coins on the ground.

walkthrough.title: Keep Your Inventory Lean
walkthrough.text: With only 6 resource slots, your inventory fills up fast. Every time you are about to take an action that generates resources, check if you have room. If not, sell something via the Shipping Bin or Pierre's, donate it, or gift it. Do not let a full inventory force you to discard something valuable just because you were not paying attention. Think of inventory management as a constant background task.

walkthrough.title: Read Grandpa's Goals Immediately
walkthrough.text: At the start of the game, study all 4 Goals carefully. Some Goals require work across the entire year (like reaching Mine Level 12 or befriending enough villagers). Others can be rushed at the end (like accumulating Gold). Knowing which Goals need early investment versus late sprints helps you plan the entire arc of the game. Do not wait until Fall to realize you needed to start the Mine in Spring.

walkthrough.title: Specialize by Profession
walkthrough.text: Each player's Profession gives them unique Upgrades throughout the game. Lean into your strengths. If your Profession supports farming, focus on crops and animals. If it supports mining, be the one pushing deeper underground. Specialization lets the team cover more ground than if everyone does a little of everything. Of course, be flexible when the situation demands it, but having a primary role keeps things efficient.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Season Deck
walkthrough.text: The Season Deck is your game clock. Count how many cards are left and plan accordingly. Each season has about 5 cards (4 regular or festival cards plus 1 Season End card). When you are in the final season, every round matters enormously. If you are behind on Bundles or Goals, you may need to take riskier or more aggressive actions. But if you planned well and communicated throughout, those final Winter rounds are where all your preparation pays off beautifully.

summary.title: Communicate Constantly
bullets: Use the Planning Phase to share resources, needs, and intentions
Coordinate who goes where to avoid duplicating efforts

summary.title: Prioritize Hearts Early
bullets: Hearts reveal Bundles, pet animals, and remove Joja Tiles
Make friends early for recurring Gift Ability triggers

summary.title: Do Not Neglect the Mine
bullets: Stone, Ore, Geodes, Bug Meat, and Items all come from the Mine
Send at least one player regularly, even if reaching Level 12 is not a Goal

summary.title: Forage Whenever You Move
bullets: Free pickup when moving along a path: 1 Forageable or 1 Tree
Never waste a movement without grabbing something

summary.title: Keep Your Inventory Lean
bullets: Only 6 resource slots; sell, donate, or gift before gaining more
A full inventory means forced, often wasteful, discards

summary.title: Read Grandpa's Goals Immediately
bullets: Some Goals need early investment (Mine, friends); others can be rushed late (Gold)
Plan the entire year's arc from the first round

summary.title: Specialize by Profession
bullets: Lean into your Profession's strengths for efficiency
Cover more ground as a team through specialization

summary.title: Watch the Season Deck
bullets: Count remaining cards to gauge urgency
Winter rounds are precious; every action counts

walkthrough.title: Map Card Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Experienced players learn to read the Map Card grid and plan Mine expeditions around favorable symbol positions. Before rolling, check which row-column combinations yield Staircases, Ore, or Items, and compare to which combinations hit Monsters. Sometimes it is worth delaying a Mine visit until the next Map Card is more favorable. When you descend, the new Map Card changes the whole equation, so factor that into your long-term Mine strategy.

walkthrough.title: Bundle Scouting and Replacement
walkthrough.text: Revealing a Bundle costs Hearts equal to the player count, and replacing one costs the same again plus any donated progress is lost. However, sometimes an impossible or very difficult Bundle is worse than the Heart cost of replacing it. Advanced players reveal Bundles early in the game so they can assess feasibility and replace before investing too many resources. The Bulletin Board Bundles are especially tricky because they require both Hearts and resources. Reveal those first to plan around them.

walkthrough.title: Fishing Economy and Legendary Fish
walkthrough.text: Fishing can be incredibly efficient if you target the right fish. Legendary Fish count toward any fishing Bundle and sell for high Gold values. They are also the only way to complete the 'Catch Legendary Fish' Goal. Since Legendary Fish return to the bag from Fish Track movement instead of being discarded, they cycle back. Position your fisher at the location that has the most matching fish on the track to maximize catch probability. Stock up on Bug Meat for Crab Pot Fish too, since those are guaranteed catches.

walkthrough.title: Crop Timing and Quality
walkthrough.text: Quality crops (flipped to the star side) sell for more Gold and satisfy certain Pantry Bundles. Rain on Season Cards waters crops for free, but you cannot predict when rain will appear. Plant crops with low watering numbers (field 1 or 2) when you need quick harvests, and higher numbers (field 3-5) when you can afford to wait for rain or multiple watering actions. Time your planting with the season: do not plant a crop that needs 4 waterings when there are only 2 Season Cards left in the current season. Crops survive season changes but cannot benefit from seasonal bonuses out of season.

walkthrough.title: Action Economy: Two Actions Are Never Enough
walkthrough.text: The biggest constraint in Stardew Valley is actions. You only get 2 per turn, and each round flies by. The best players maximize every action. Foraging during movement is free value. The Spouse Pawn adds a third action per turn. Some Profession Upgrades and Items grant bonus actions or free effects. The Marriage mechanic is one of the most powerful strategies in the game: prioritize obtaining the Mermaid's Pendant and marrying a villager with a strong Gift Ability. That extra action per turn compounds massively over the remaining game.

walkthrough.title: Heart Token Economy
walkthrough.text: Hearts flow through nearly every system in the game: revealing Bundles, petting animals to Happy, removing Joja Tiles. Advanced players carefully budget their Heart expenditure. Early Hearts should go toward revealing all Bundles so you can plan. Mid-game Hearts can pet animals for Quality products. Late-game Hearts should be reserved for Joja removal if playing Artisan. Gift Abilities that generate Hearts each season are among the most valuable in the game; prioritize befriending villagers with strong gift abilities.

walkthrough.title: Building Priority Order
walkthrough.text: Buildings cost Wood, Stone, and Gold, which are all tight resources. Prioritize the Coop or Barn first if 'Have 2 animals per player' is a Goal, since you cannot buy animals without them. Other Buildings provide powerful passive effects. Evaluate which Buildings are available (only 2 random ones plus Coop and Barn) and see which aligns with your Goals. If 'Build 1 Building per player' is a Goal, plan your resource gathering early. Remember that the Mountain location is where you buy Buildings, so whoever goes there should be loaded with materials.

walkthrough.title: Artisan Mode: Managing the Joja Threat
walkthrough.text: In Artisan mode, you must clear every Joja Tile on the board to win. This changes the entire calculus. Every Joja icon on a Season Card now represents a direct threat. You need a steady flow of Hearts or Gold specifically for Joja removal. Assign one player as the Joja Watcher who tracks tiles and prioritizes removal during End of Turn. Pair this with friend-making to keep Heart generation high. On particularly Joja-heavy Season Decks, you may need to sacrifice other objectives temporarily to keep the board clean.

walkthrough.title: Profession Synergies
walkthrough.text: Each Profession has unique Upgrades that unlock at Season End. The best teams create synergies between their Professions. A farmer focused on crops pairs well with a miner who provides Ore for tool upgrades. A fisher catching Crab Pot Fish and Legendary Fish pairs with a socializer generating Hearts. Study the Profession Upgrade cards when you receive them and communicate which Upgrades you kept. Sometimes discarding an existing Upgrade for a more situationally useful one mid-game is the right call.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Sequencing
walkthrough.text: In the final season, victory often comes down to sequencing your last few turns perfectly. Work backward from your win condition. If you need 2 more Bundle donations and 1 more Goal, figure out exactly which player needs to be at which location with which resources. Sometimes the optimal play is for one player to sell everything for Gold while another rushes to the Community Center. Communicate your exact plan, count your remaining Season Cards, and execute. The Valley rewards teams who finish strong, and there is no sweeter feeling than completing that final Goal with one card left in the deck.

summary.title: Map Card Manipulation
bullets: Study the Map Card grid before rolling to identify best/worst outcomes
Time Mine visits around favorable Map Cards

summary.title: Bundle Scouting and Replacement
bullets: Reveal Bundles early to assess feasibility
Replace impossible Bundles before wasting resources on donations

summary.title: Fishing Economy and Legendary Fish
bullets: Legendary Fish work for any fishing Bundle and cycle back through the bag
Stock Bug Meat for guaranteed Crab Pot catches

summary.title: Crop Timing and Quality
bullets: Low watering number = fast harvest; high number = needs rain or multiple waterings
Quality crops satisfy Pantry Bundles and sell for more Gold

summary.title: Action Economy: Two Actions Are Never Enough
bullets: Forage for free during movement; pursue Marriage for a bonus third action
Profession Upgrades and Items that grant extra actions are top-tier

summary.title: Heart Token Economy
bullets: Budget Hearts: early = reveal Bundles, mid = pet animals, late = remove Joja
Prioritize befriending villagers with strong Gift Abilities

summary.title: Building Priority Order
bullets: Coop/Barn first if animal Goals are in play
Evaluate random Buildings for alignment with active Goals

summary.title: Artisan Mode: Managing the Joja Threat
bullets: Assign a Joja Watcher to prioritize tile removal
Maintain steady Heart/Gold flow for clearing tiles

summary.title: Profession Synergies
bullets: Build team composition around complementary Professions
Communicate which Upgrades you keep each season

summary.title: Endgame Sequencing
bullets: Work backward from win conditions in the final season
Coordinate exact locations and resources for the last few turns

entries.definition: Three custom dice with faces showing Heart, Junimo, and Stardrop symbols. Used for fishing, mining, and opening geodes.
Dice rolled when using the Collect from Animals action. Each face shows an animal type icon; matching icons activate owned animals to produce resources.
The first player marker. Passed to the left whenever the Pet Wanders icon appears on a Season Card. The player holding it is the Starting Player.
The organized tray holding resource tiles for crops, animal products, bug meat, stones, ore, and geodes. Tiles are drawn from here when gained.
The blue cloth bag containing all Fish tiles, including Regular Fish, Crab Pot Fish, Legendary Fish, Trash, and Treasure Chests. Draw from it to refill the Fish Track.
The gray cloth bag containing Artifact and Mineral tiles. Draw from it when opening geodes, finding dig spots, or triggered by Mine events.
Each player's personal board showing their Profession, slots for 6 resources, 2 items, 2 Profession Upgrades, and their Starting Tool.
The deck of Season Cards representing the game year. Contains Regular, Festival, and Season End cards for Spring, Summer, Fall, and Winter. When exhausted, the game ends.
A special Season Card that triggers the transition between seasons. Swaps forageables and trees, grants Profession Upgrades, then the next Season Card is drawn immediately.
Four objective cards revealed at the start of the game. All 4 must be completed to win. Examples include reaching Mine Level 12, having a certain number of friends, or accumulating Gold.
The central building on the board with 6 rooms. Each room is restored by completing its Bundle. Restoring all 6 rooms is required to win on Honest Farmer and Artisan difficulty.
A card placed face down on a Community Center room. Once revealed (by spending Hearts), it shows what resources must be donated to restore the room. Donations required equal the number of players.
The antagonist of the game. Joja Tiles placed on the board block or weaken actions. If the game ends without meeting objectives, Joja wins.
A tile drawn when the Joja icon appears on a Season Card. Placed on a board action, it alters or blocks that action until removed by spending 1 Heart or 5 Gold.
A communal resource shared by all players. Earned by befriending villagers. Spent to reveal Bundles, pet animals, and remove Joja Tiles. No cap on total Hearts held.
Communal currency shared by all players. Earned by selling resources. Spent on seeds, animals, buildings, and removing Joja Tiles. Comes in 1, 5, and 10 denominations.

Double-sided resource tile representing a farmable crop. Normal side up when planted; Quality side (star) is flipped by Season Card icons or special abilities. Planted into fields matching the watering number.
The reverse side of a Crop or Animal Product tile, denoted by a star. Quality items sell for more Gold and are required by certain Bundles.
Tiles scattered face down on the board each season. Collected for free when moving along adjacent paths. Can be gifted, donated to Bundles, or sold.
A material gained by collecting Tree tiles during movement. Used as a building material for constructing Buildings at Robin's shop.
A material gained from the Mine. Used for building construction and for building staircases to descend deeper in the Mine (spend Stones equal to player count).
A resource gained from the Mine. Types available depend on the current Mine Level. Used for tool upgrades, Bundles, and crafting.
A resource gained from the Mine. Opened at the Forge by rolling a Stardew Die. Can yield Stone, Ore, Minerals, Artifacts, or Items.
A rare tile drawn from the gray bag. Can be donated to the Museum in a matching lettered slot (A-H). Filling a column of the Museum grants an Epic Item.
A tile drawn from the gray bag, similar to Artifacts. Donated to Museum slots matching their letter. Useful for Museum Goals and certain Bundles.
A resource gained from the Mine. Spent to catch Crab Pot Fish (1 Bug Meat per Crab Pot Fish). A handy alternative to dice-based fishing.
Resources generated by owned animals (Milk, Eggs, Wool, etc.). Produced by rolling the Animal Dice. Happy animals produce Quality products.
The row of 5 slots on the board holding current Fish tiles. Fish are caught from here. The track shifts and refills via Season Card icons and after fishing actions.
Four rare fish in the Fishing Bag. Caught like normal fish. Never discarded from Fish Track movement (returned to bag instead). Can be donated to any fishing Bundle.
Special fish caught by spending Bug Meat instead of dice. One Bug Meat per Crab Pot Fish. Caught in addition to your normal dice-based fishing during the same action.
A tile on the Fish Track. If you catch the fish immediately to its left, discard the Treasure Chest and gain an Item card.
A tile on the Fish Track with no inherent value. If you catch nothing during a fishing action, you may take a Trash tile to clear a slot. Can be discarded from inventory at any time.
Cards numbered 1-12 stacked on the board. Shows available Ore and Geode types, and the Monster Ability for that level. Deeper levels have better resources and scarier monsters.
A grid card placed next to the Mine Levels. When exploring the Mine, roll 2 Stardew Dice and use one for the row and one for the column to find your result on the grid.
A Mine result that lets you descend to the next level. Reveal the next Mine Level Card and draw a new Map Card. Can also be built as an End of Turn Effect by spending Stones equal to player count.
An encounter triggered by skull results on the Map Card. The Monster Ability on the current Mine Level Card determines the negative effect. Gets worse at deeper levels.
A card representing a Valley resident. Shows loved gifts, hated gifts, birthday season, and a Gift Ability. Kept beside your Player Mat when befriended.
A special power on a Villager card that activates when a Gift icon appears on a Season Card. Each player may trigger 1 befriended villager's Gift Ability per Gift event.
A resource type pictured on a Villager card that earns 2 Heart Tokens when gifted to that villager. The best way to maximize Heart gain from friendships.
A resource type shown on the Villager card that cannot be gifted to that villager at all. Marked distinctly to avoid confusion.
If the current game season matches a villager's birthday season (shown on their card), you earn 1 extra Heart Token when gifting them. A nice reason to time your friendships.
A special pawn gained by marrying a villager using the Mermaid's Pendant. Placed during Planning Phase. Takes 1 action at its location during your turn, using your inventory.
An Epic Item required for marriage. Attach it to a befriended villager with the marriage icon to get married and gain use of the Spouse Pawn.
Common items gained from various sources (Mine, fishing, Bundles). Each player may hold up to 2. Some have passive abilities, others have 'Discard to Use' effects.
Rare and powerful items with no inventory limit. Earned by completing Bundles (the 6th grants one) and by filling Museum columns. Cannot always be traded.
Each player's specialization, determined by their Player Mat. Influences which Profession Upgrade cards they receive at each Season End.
Special ability cards drawn at Season End. Each player draws 2 matching their Profession and keeps 1 (max 2 held). Cannot be traded. Some have Gift Abilities.
Each player chooses one of 4 tools (Watering Can, Hoe, Fishing Rod, Pickaxe) at the start. Begins at Level 0 and upgrades by discarding listed resources as an End of Turn Effect.
The easiest difficulty. Win by completing 4 of Grandpa's Goals only. Community Center Bundles are not required. Great for learning the game.
Standard difficulty. Win by completing 4 of Grandpa's Goals AND restoring all 6 Community Center Rooms. The intended experience for most groups.
The hardest difficulty. Win by completing Goals, restoring the Community Center, AND having no Joja Tiles remaining on the board. For experienced groups seeking a challenge.
Located at the Forge. Donate Artifacts and Minerals into lettered slots (A-H). Filling a column of 4 slots grants an Epic Item. Up to 2 Epic Items available from the Museum.
A Town action where you buy seeds (1 Gold each, current season only) and sell resources for Gold. The main way to plant crops and convert resources to currency.
A Town action where you reveal face-down Bundles by spending Hearts and donate resources to complete revealed Bundles. The central objective location.
A Mountain action for buying Buildings. Spend Gold, Wood, and Stone to construct available Building tiles, which grant passive abilities or unlock animal purchases.
The Ranch location action for buying animals. Requires the Coop or Barn building first. Multiple animals can be purchased in a single action if you have enough Gold.
A Forge action for opening Geodes. Roll 1 Stardew Die per Geode and consult the chart on the board. Results include Stone, specific Ore, Minerals, Artifacts, or Items.
A Season Card icon that allows all players to trade and sell resources for Gold. One of the main opportunities to convert surplus resources into currency.
A Season Card icon that moves all planted crops 1 space to the right, just like the Water Crops action. Crops that move off the track are harvested by the Starting Player.
A Season Card icon (green or red) that forces you to discard 1 crop from a field matching the crow's color. Field 3 counts as both green and red.
After completing your actions and returning to the Farmhouse, choose 1 type (Build Staircase, Pet Animals, Remove Joja, or Upgrade Tool) and repeat it as many times as able.
An animal flipped to its Happy side by spending Heart Tokens (End of Turn Effect). Happy animals produce Quality animal products when activated by the Animal Dice.
A Building tile required to buy Coop animals (Chickens, Ducks, Rabbits, Dinosaurs). Must be constructed at Robin's before any Coop animals can be purchased.
A Building tile required to buy Barn animals (Cows, Goats, Sheep, Pigs). Must be constructed at Robin's before any Barn animals can be purchased.
A magical forest spirit depicted on Stardew Dice faces and various cards. In the board game, Junimo is one of the three die symbols used for fishing and mining.
A mystical fruit from Stardew Valley lore. In the board game, it appears as one of the three Stardew Dice symbols alongside Heart and Junimo.
